articles.title: Bright lights and hot orbs: UFO files shed light on sightings but leave interpretation to the public
articles.summary: Newly released Pentagon files include an internal FBI memo dated October 19, 1966 that reports witnesses in the 1960s saw three‑ to four‑foot‑tall beings in space suits and helmets landing from unidentified flying objects. S. Air Force withheld information to avoid mass panic, and described UFOs as polished metal objects that could move silently, hover, and emit bright lights.

en.summary: Newly released Pentagon files include an internal FBI memo dated October 19, 1966 that reports witnesses in the 1960s saw three‑ to four‑foot‑tall beings in space suits and helmets landing from unidentified flying objects. de.title: Helle Lichter und heiße Kugeln: UFO-Akten geben Aufschluss über Sichtungen, überlassen die Interpretation jedoch der Öffentlichkeit
de.summary: Zu den neu veröffentlichten Pentagon-Akten gehört ein internes FBI-Memo vom 19. Oktober 1966, in dem es heißt, Zeugen hätten in den 1960er Jahren drei bis vier Fuß große Wesen in Raumanzügen und Helmen gesehen, die von unbekannten Flugobjekten landeten. Die S. Air Force hielt Informationen zurück, um eine Massenpanik zu vermeiden, und beschrieb UFOs als polierte Metallobjekte, die sich lautlos bewegen, schweben und helles Licht ausstrahlen könnten.

articles.title: Secret agent, who was about to blow the lid off UFO secrets, died 5 years ago. A whistleblower has now raised suspicion
articles.summary: Kevin Childress, a 30‑year Department of Energy criminal investigator, died unexpectedly in 2021 at his Georgia home, with authorities citing complications from COVID‑19. UFO whistleblower Luis Elizondo claims Childress was healthy before his death and had warned that the DOE had a significant role in UAP programs. Elizondo says the DOE is trying to silence him and notes that no public autopsy or detailed cause of death has been released.

en.title: Secret agent, who was about to blow the lid off UFO secrets, died 5 years ago. A whistleblower has now raised suspicion
en.summary: Kevin Childress, a 30‑year Department of Energy criminal investigator, died unexpectedly in 2021 at his Georgia home, with authorities citing complications from COVID‑19. UFO whistleblower Luis Elizondo claims Childress was healthy before his death and had warned that the DOE had a significant role in UAP programs. Elizondo says the DOE is trying to silence him and notes that no public autopsy or detailed cause of death has been released.

de.title: Der Geheimagent, der UFO-Geheimnisse enthüllen wollte, starb vor fünf Jahren. Ein Whistleblower hat nun Verdacht geäußert
de.summary: Kevin Childress, ein 30-jähriger Kriminalermittler des Energieministeriums, starb 2021 unerwartet in seinem Haus in Georgia, wobei die Behörden Komplikationen durch COVID-19 anführten. UFO-Whistleblower Luis Elizondo behauptet, Childress sei vor seinem Tod gesund gewesen und habe gewarnt, dass das DOE eine wichtige Rolle in UAP-Programmen gespielt habe. Elizondo sagt, das Energieministerium versuche ihn zum Schweigen zu bringen und stellt fest, dass weder eine öffentliche Autopsie noch eine detaillierte Todesursache veröffentlicht wurden.

de.title: UFO-Dateien | Auf der Suche nach Kuchen im Himmel
de.summary: Das US-Kriegsministerium hat auf seiner Website einen ersten Stapel von 161 freigegebenen UFO/UAP-Dateien veröffentlicht, von denen einige aus den 1940er Jahren stammen. Präsident Donald Trump kündigte die Veröffentlichung auf X an und nannte sie einen Schritt hin zu „vollständiger und maximaler Transparenz“ über außerirdische und außerirdische Phänomene.

summaries.file-release-bright-lights-and-hot-orbs-: Newly released Pentagon files include an internal FBI memo dated October 19, 1966 that reports witnesses in the 1960s saw three‑ to four‑foot‑tall beings in space suits and helmets landing from unidentified flying objects. The memo cites the book *Flying Saucers – Serious Business* by Frank Edwards, who claimed UFOs were space vehicles sent to observe Earth, that the U.S. Air Force withheld information to avoid mass panic, and described UFOs as polished metal objects that could move silently, hover, and emit bright lights. Witnesses named in the memo are described as reliable, including law enforcement officers, military personnel, and commercial airline pilots, and wreckage from at least three crashed objects—magnesium alloy, pure magnesium, and an unknown hard metal with 15‑micron spheres—has been recovered. The memo ends with Edwards predicting an imminent overt landing or deliberate contact by UFOs, and a trove of 162 UFO files, including photographs, video footage, and a 1947 flying‑disc sighting, was released to the public, with the Trump administration saying more documents will be released gradually.
summaries.film-sleeping-dog-how-new-ufo-documentar: Journalist and filmmaker Jeremy Corbell discusses his new documentary with NBC News correspondent Gadi Schwartz. The documentary is titled “Sleeping Dog.” It explores Corbell’s navigation of government secrecy. It also covers whistleblowers and his search for truth about UFOs.
summaries.ufo-controversy-reignites-after-former-c: Former CIA‑funded researcher Dr. Hal Puthoff said the United States has recovered four distinct species of extraterrestrial life from crashed UFOs, although he has not seen the evidence himself. He cited unnamed individuals involved in recoveries who claim the species are Grays, Nordics, Insectoids, and Reptilians, as identified by Dr. Eric Davis. Davis described the species as humanoid with two arms and legs, with Nordics and Reptilians about six feet tall, Nordics resembling northern Europeans, Reptilians lizard‑like with tails, Grays small and hairless, and Insectoids mantis‑like. In 2023, former Air Force intelligence officer David Grusch testified before Congress that the U.S. possessed “non‑human biologics” from several crashed UFOs, citing testimony from people in the alleged retrieval program. Both Puthoff and Farah have said that those involved in recoveries have not publicly spoken on camera, leaving the claims unverified.
summaries.secret-agent-who-was-about-to-blow-the-l: Kevin Childress, a 30‑year Department of Energy criminal investigator, died unexpectedly in 2021 at his Georgia home, with authorities citing complications from COVID‑19. UFO whistleblower Luis Elizondo claims Childress was healthy before his death and had warned that the DOE had a significant role in UAP programs. Elizondo says the DOE is trying to silence him and notes that no public autopsy or detailed cause of death has been released. The FBI is investigating a series of missing and dead scientists linked to secret nuclear and UFO programs, including the disappearance of retired General William Neil McCasland. Other scientists, such as David Wilcock, have also died under suspicious circumstances, adding to the pattern of unexplained losses.
summaries.ufo-files-in-pursuit-of-pies-in-the-sky: The U.S. Department of War released a first batch of 161 declassified UFO/UAP files, some dating back to the 1940s, on its website. President Donald Trump announced the release on X, calling it a move toward “complete and maximum transparency” about alien and extraterrestrial phenomena. The files contain reports from the FBI, State Department, NASA, and Pentagon, including images of flying discs, a football‑shaped UAP near Japan, a 2026 U.S. Army sighting, and a glowing orange orb likened to the Eye of Sauron. A March 2024 Pentagon report noted that no evidence proved UAP were alien technology, with many sightings later identified as weather balloons, spy planes, satellites or other normal activity.

de.summary: Das US-Kriegsministerium hat auf seiner Website einen ersten Stapel von 161 freigegebenen UFO/UAP-Dateien veröffentlicht, von denen einige aus den 1940er Jahren stammen. Präsident Donald Trump kündigte die Veröffentlichung auf X an und nannte sie einen Schritt hin zu „vollständiger und maximaler Transparenz“ über außerirdische und außerirdische Phänomene.

translationMeta.target: de for English articles, en for German articles
